text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: [on file]
ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
DICTATED BY: [GU Oncology Fellow / Attending]

---

REASON FOR VISIT:
Routine follow-up and ongoing management of metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) on cabozantinib therapy. Patient presents for her approximately 9-month check on cabozantinib, coinciding with evaluation of a new musculoskeletal complaint.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old female with a known diagnosis of Stage IV (cT3bN2M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (22% of tumor volume), initially presenting with an 11.4 cm right renal mass with renal vein and infrarenal IVC involvement (Level I-II thrombus), retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (9/14 nodes positive at resection), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Germline testing was negative for hereditary RCC syndromes. Molecular profiling demonstrated VHL loss-of-function mutation, PBRM1 splice-site mutation, SETD2 frameshift mutation, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; PD-L1 CPS was 55 (high) by 22C3 immunohistochemistry. IMDC intermediate-risk group.

She underwent open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection and IVC thrombectomy without major complication. Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3b N2 (9/14 positive) ccRCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4, sarcomatoid features 22%, with extranodal extension in the largest involved node.

Approximately 8 weeks post-operatively, she began first-line systemic therapy with Yervoy (1 mg/kg IV q3w x 4 doses) plus Opdivo (3 mg/kg IV q3w for induction, then Opdivo 480 mg IV q4w for maintenance), per the CheckMate 214 regimen. Her course was complicated by immune-related grade 2 colitis (managed with held ipi and oral budesonide, which ultimately resolved) and immune-related grade 2 hypothyroidism (managed with levothyroxine, now continued indefinitely at 100 mcg/day). She achieved a partial response at first restaging (approximately -42% sum of target lesion diameters).

At approximately 6 months of therapy, she developed mixed response with enlargement of the right lower lobe pulmonary lesion and appearance of a new right adrenal nodule. CT-guided biopsy of the adrenal confirmed metastatic ccRCC. She underwent SBRT to the right lower lobe lesion (54 Gy in 3 fractions) with local control achieved on subsequent imaging. However, at restaging approximately 9 months into first-line therapy, systemic progressive disease was documented: multiple pulmonary lesions had grown, a new hepatic segment VI lesion appeared (1.6 cm), and the adrenal lesion had slightly increased. She was transitioned to second-line therapy with cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg/day.

Cabozantinib was dose-reduced to 40 mg/day after approximately 3 weeks due to concurrent Grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (HFS) and Grade 2 diarrhea. Antihypertensive regimen was augmented (lisinopril 10 mg/day added to amlodipine 10 mg/day) for Grade 2 hypertension developing at approximately 6 weeks on cabozantinib. At her 3-month restaging on cabozantinib, imaging demonstrated stable disease with modest reductions across all measurable sites (approximately -16% from cabozantinib initiation). At 6-month restaging, stable disease was maintained with all lesions unchanged. She has now been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months.

Today's visit is prompted by a NEW complaint of mild right hip discomfort beginning approximately 3-4 weeks ago. She describes a dull, achy pain localized to the right groin and lateral hip region, rated approximately 2-3/10 at baseline, worsening to 4-5/10 with prolonged ambulation. There is no radiation down the leg, no associated weakness or numbness, no mechanical instability, and no history of trauma. She has not required opioid analgesics. She has been taking acetaminophen 500 mg BID with moderate benefit. Given her known metastatic disease to multiple sites, osseous metastasis was considered in the differential and a bone scan was ordered at this visit. She denies any other new musculoskeletal complaints.

With respect to her known metastatic sites and cabozantinib tolerability, she continues on cabozantinib 40 mg/day without further dose reductions since the initial adjustment. She reports her overall quality of life remains acceptable and she is managing independently within her home with support from her daughter.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2, overall somewhat improved from the period of immunotherapy progression. No fevers, no chills, no unintentional weight loss (weight stable at today's visit compared to prior). Mild decreased appetite attributed to cabozantinib, manageable.
Head/Eyes/ENT: No headache, no visual changes, no oral ulcers or mucositis (she was counseled previously regarding cabozantinib-related stomatitis; she has had none).
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema. Blood pressure managed as below.
Pulmonary: Mild dyspnea on exertion with moderate activity (climbing one flight of stairs); she attributes this to deconditioning and known pulmonary metastases. No hemoptysis, no pleuritic pain, no cough.
Gastrointestinal: Diarrhea 1-2 loose stools above her personal baseline per day (grade 1); denies frank blood or mucus in stool. Mild lower abdominal cramping intermittently. Nausea grade 1, no vomiting. Appetite mildly reduced.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. Urinary frequency at baseline. (Single functioning left kidney; creatinine and eGFR monitored closely.)
Musculoskeletal: Right hip/groin discomfort as described. No back pain. No other joint complaints.
Skin: Bilateral palms and plantar surfaces of feet with persistent faint erythema and mild hyperkeratosis, right heel most prominently affected — consistent with ongoing grade 1 HFS. No new blistering, no skin breakdown at this time. No rash elsewhere.
Neurological: No focal neurological deficits. No headaches, no confusion, no vision changes. (MRI brain not repeated at this visit; last brain MRI was negative for intracranial metastases at approximately 9 months into first-line therapy.)
Endocrine/Metabolic: Cold intolerance and prior hypothyroid symptoms resolved on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day. No palpitations, no tremor.
Psychological: She reports mild anxiety related to disease uncertainty; engaged with palliative care team and finding their involvement helpful. No clinical depression per her report.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS (relevant):
1. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 100 mcg orally once daily (on empty stomach)
3. Amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension; also nephroprotective for single kidney)
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily (hyperlipidemia)
6. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally BID PRN pain (current use for right hip)
7. Loperamide 2 mg orally PRN (diarrhea, used occasionally)
8. Urea cream 40% topically to bilateral palms and plantar surfaces twice daily (HFS management)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

RELEVANT PAST HISTORY (established):
- Stage IV ccRCC as above
- Hypertension (antedating cancer diagnosis; managed with amlodipine, now also lisinopril)
- Hyperlipidemia (on statin)
- Immune-related hypothyroidism (ICI-related, on levothyroxine indefinitely)
- Prior immune-related colitis (ICI-related, resolved; budesonide taper completed)
- History of IVC/renal vein tumor thrombus (surgically addressed)
- Remote tobacco history, 20 pack-years, quit at age 48
- Single functioning left kidney (post-right radical nephrectomy)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: BP 134/82 mmHg (left arm, seated), HR 78 bpm (regular), RR 16 breaths/min, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight: [on file], Height: [on file], Temperature: 36.8°C.
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Appropriately dressed and groomed. Pleasant and engaged in conversation.
Head/Eyes/ENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric, conjunctivae non-pale. Oropharynx clear without mucositis or ulceration.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged on palpation.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No peripheral edema in bilateral lower extremities.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion. Chest wall expansion symmetric.
Abdominal: Well-healed midline laparotomy scar from prior nephrectomy/RPLND/IVC thrombectomy. Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No abdominal masses appreciated.
Musculoskeletal: Right hip — mild tenderness to palpation over the lateral greater trochanter and right groin region. Range of motion of the right hip intact but limited by discomfort at the extremes of internal rotation and abduction. No crepitus. No swelling or warmth. Gait mildly antalgic favoring the right side. Log roll test: mildly positive for right hip pain. Straight leg raise: negative bilaterally (no radicular component).
Skin: Bilateral palmar surfaces with faint erythema and mild hyperkeratosis, greatest at the proximal palmar creases; right heel with focal hyperkeratotic plaque, approximately 1.5 cm, without active ulceration or blistering. Consistent with grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. No rash, petechiae, or jaundice.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch throughout. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No focal neurological deficits.
Lymph nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy on palpation.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):

Complete Blood Count:
- Hgb 10.2 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable; likely multifactorial — chronic disease, prior surgery, single kidney)
- WBC 6.8 x10^9/L (normal differential)
- Platelets 201 x10^9/L (normal)
- MCV 88 fL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.2 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L
- BUN 22 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (stable, baseline for single-kidney post-nephrectomy)
- eGFR approximately 54 mL/min/1.73m2 (stable; no acute kidney injury)
- AST 38 U/L (normal), ALT 36 U/L (normal; improvement from elevated levels seen at time of hepatic metastasis detection)
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L (mild improvement from prior 124 U/L)
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL, Albumin 3.9 g/dL (normal)
- Calcium 9.2 mg/dL (normal; reassuring against lytic/blastic bone metastasis)
- Glucose 96 mg/dL (fasting, normal)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH 2.5 mIU/L (within therapeutic range on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day; no change to dose at this time)
- Free T4: not repeated today (TSH therapeutic)

Urinalysis:
- Trace protein (1+), no blood, no WBCs, no casts — stable trace proteinuria on lisinopril (prior 24-hour urine protein 680 mg/24h, below threshold)

Lipid panel: Deferred to primary care.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Bone Scan (ordered this visit, result available today or per nursing protocol for results communication):
A whole-body radionuclide bone scan (technetium-99m MDP) was performed to evaluate the right hip complaint and rule out osseous metastasis. Result: NEGATIVE FOR BONE METASTASIS. Mild increased uptake in the right hip region consistent with degenerative changes. No focal areas of abnormal osseous uptake to suggest metastatic involvement. Incidental mild degenerative uptake at bilateral knee joints. No pathological fracture or impending fracture identified.

Most recent restaging CT (approximately 6 months of cabozantinib): Stable disease maintained — pulmonary lesions (right upper lobe 1.4 cm, left lower lobe 1.1 cm, left upper lobe 0.7 cm), hepatic segment VI lesion 1.2 cm, right adrenal 1.2 cm; right lower lobe SBRT-treated lesion 1.3 cm (fibrous/treated). No new lesions at that time. Next restaging CT is scheduled in approximately 3 months.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, sarcomatoid features (22%), pT3bN2M1 — STABLE DISEASE on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg/day.
   - Patient has been on cabozantinib for approximately 9 months. Last formal CT restaging confirmed stable disease at the 6-month mark. Next restaging CT is due in approximately 3 months. No clinical or laboratory evidence of acute progression at this visit.
   - Trajectory is reassuring given initially aggressive disease (sarcomatoid differentiation, multiple nodal metastases with extranodal extension, IVC thrombus, subsequent hepatic and adrenal spread). Prolonged disease control on cabozantinib is a meaningful clinical benefit.

2. Right hip pain — BENIGN (osteoarthritis) per bone scan; osseous metastasis excluded.
   - Bone scan negative for metastatic disease. Findings consistent with right hip osteoarthritis with trochanteric bursitis or early degenerative hip disease, given the clinical exam findings (greater trochanteric tenderness, limited ROM at extremes without neurological involvement). This is likely exacerbated by reduced physical activity and mild deconditioning from prolonged cancer treatment. No pathological fracture risk identified.
   - Note: cabozantinib inhibits MET/AXL pathways that may have some theoretical bone-protective effects; osseous metastasis in ccRCC, while reported, is less common in her current disease distribution.

3. Palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (HFS), grade 1 — STABLE, managed conservatively.
   - Persistent but not worsening. No grade 2 recurrence since the initial dose reduction. Right heel most prominently affected with focal hyperkeratotic plaque; no active ulceration.

4. Diarrhea, grade 1, cabozantinib-related — STABLE.
   - 1-2 loose stools above baseline per day. No significant impact on daily function. Managed with dietary modification and PRN loperamide.

5. Fatigue, grade 1-2 — ongoing, multifactorial (underlying malignancy, prior surgery, chronic mild anemia, cabozantinib).

6. Immune checkpoint inhibitor-related hypothyroidism — WELL-CONTROLLED on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day (TSH 2.5 mIU/L, therapeutic).

7. Hypertension — AT GOAL on amlodipine 10 mg + lisinopril 10 mg daily. BP 134/82 mmHg today.

8. Mild normocytic anemia — STABLE (Hgb 10.2 g/dL). Likely chronic disease anemia compounded by single kidney, prior surgery. No evidence of active bleeding. LFTs normal. No iron deficiency documented.

9. Chronic kidney disease, mild (eGFR ~54 mL/min), single kidney — STABLE. Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, stable. Lisinopril continued for nephroprotection. Urinalysis with trace proteinuria — monitoring.

---

PLAN:

1. CABOZANTINIB: Continue at 40 mg/day. No dose modification indicated at this visit. Toxicities are manageable at current dose; patient tolerating well. Counsel patient to contact clinic immediately for worsening HFS (blistering, inability to walk), new diarrhea grade 2+ (6+ loose stools/day), hypertensive urgency, or any new neurological symptoms, visual changes, or severe fatigue.

2. RIGHT HIP PAIN: Bone scan negative for metastasis — reassuring. Diagnosis consistent with right hip osteoarthritis and likely trochanteric bursitis. Recommendations:
   - Continue acetaminophen 500-1000 mg BID-TID as needed for pain management (avoid NSAIDs given single kidney and lisinopril use — risk of acute kidney injury).
   - Referral to physical therapy for hip-targeted strengthening and range-of-motion program; will also address general deconditioning.
   - Orthopedic or sports medicine referral offered if pain does not improve with conservative management within 6-8 weeks.
   - Patient instructed: if pain worsens significantly, becomes constant, awakens her from sleep, or is associated with any new neurological symptoms, she should contact the clinic promptly for repeat evaluation (including MRI of the right hip/pelvis if clinically indicated).

3. THYROID: TSH 2.5 mIU/L — therapeutic. Levothyroxine 100 mcg/day unchanged. Recheck TSH at next visit in approximately 3 months, as cabozantinib can affect thyroid function through VEGFR-mediated mechanisms in addition to baseline ICI-related hypothyroidism.

4. HYPERTENSION: BP at goal (134/82 mmHg). Continue amlodipine 10 mg/day + lisinopril 10 mg/day. Renal function and electrolytes monitored at each visit.

5. ANEMIA: Hgb 10.2 g/dL, stable. Reticulocyte count not repeated today (trend stable). Monitoring ongoing. No erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) initiated (no indication at this level). If Hgb falls below 8.5 g/dL or patient becomes symptomatic, iron studies and further hematologic evaluation will be obtained.

6. HFS (grade 1): Continue urea cream 40% twice daily to bilateral palms and plantar surfaces. Continue use of padded insoles, silicone heel cups (right heel), and soft footwear. Reinforce avoidance of heat, tight footwear, and friction.

7. DIARRHEA (grade 1): Continue dietary modification (low-residue diet during flares, increased soluble fiber at baseline). PRN loperamide as needed. Discuss with patient to maintain adequate hydration.

8. RESTAGING IMAGING: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast scheduled in approximately 3 months (12-month mark on cabozantinib). Given prior hepatic and adrenal involvement, the full abdomen/pelvis is included routinely. MRI brain: deferred unless neurological symptoms develop (last imaging was negative; no new indications).

9. PALLIATIVE CARE CO-MANAGEMENT: Continue integrated palliative care involvement. Patient engaged with team; finds support helpful. Goals of care previously discussed — patient's priorities remain quality of life, independence, and family engagement. No changes to goals of care directives at this time.

10. LABS AT NEXT VISIT (approximately 3 months): CBC with differential, CMP (including LFTs, creatinine), TSH, urinalysis with microscopy.

11. PRIMARY CARE COORDINATION: Recommend primary care follow-up for annual wellness items (lipid panel, blood pressure monitoring between oncology visits, preventive care appropriate for her age and health status).

Patient verbalized understanding of plan, including the significance of the negative bone scan, the expectation of continued cabozantinib therapy, and warning signs to prompt early contact with the clinic. She expressed cautious optimism regarding her stable disease trajectory. Daughter was present and engaged in the discussion. Questions answered to satisfaction.

Return to GU Oncology clinic in approximately 3 months, or sooner as needed.

[Electronically signed by GU Medical Oncology Attending]
